section.title: Encouragement to Faithfulness
section.sectionRationale: The letter opens with Paul, an aging apostle in a Roman prison facing death, writing to his beloved son in the faith. Before any hard charge, he grounds Timothy in grace: a heritage of sincere faith, a gift of God to be stirred up, a gospel worth suffering for, and a good deposit worth guarding. The chapter moves from tender thanksgiving to a ringing call not to be ashamed, and it ends with living examples of both desertion and loyalty.
section.thesis: Because God has saved and called us by His own grace and has given us a Spirit of power, love, and self-control, we need not be ashamed of the gospel or its prisoners; instead we fan the gift into flame and guard the good deposit, trusting the One who keeps what we entrust to Him.
section.frameNote: Four movements within the chapter: the greeting and thanksgiving for Timothy's sincere faith; the call to fan the gift into flame and not shrink back in fear; the summons to share in suffering for a gospel of grace and to guard the deposit; and the contrast between those who deserted Paul and faithful Onesiphorus.

verses.text: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, according to the promise of life in Christ Jesus,
To Timothy, my beloved child: Grace, mercy, and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord.
I thank God, whom I serve with a clear conscience as did my forefathers, as I constantly remember you night and day in my prayers.
Recalling your tears, I long to see you so that I may be filled with joy.
I am reminded of your sincere faith, which first dwelt in your grandmother Lois and your mother Eunice, and I am convinced is in you as well.
For this reason I remind you to {{anazopyreo|fan into flame}} the gift of God, which is in you through the laying on of my hands.
For God has not given us a spirit of {{deilia|fear}}, but of power, love, and self-control.

units.label: Do not be ashamed of the gospel

verses.text: So do not be {{epaischynomai|ashamed}} of the testimony of our Lord, or of me, His prisoner. Instead, join me in suffering for the gospel by the power of God.
He has saved us and called us to a holy calling, not because of our works, but by His own purpose and by the grace He granted us in Christ Jesus before time began.
And now He has revealed this grace through the appearing of our Savior, Christ Jesus, who has abolished death and illuminated the way to life and immortality through the gospel,
to which I was appointed as a preacher, an apostle, and a teacher.
For this reason, even though I suffer as I do, I am not ashamed; for I know whom I have believed, and I am convinced that He is able to guard what I have entrusted to Him for that day.
Hold on to the {{hypotyposis|pattern}} of sound teaching you have heard from me, with the faith and love that are in Christ Jesus.
Guard the {{paratheke|treasure entrusted}} to you, with the help of the Holy Spirit who dwells in us.
You know that everyone in the Province of Asia has deserted me, including Phygelus and Hermogenes.
May the Lord grant mercy to the household of Onesiphorus, because he has often refreshed me and was unashamed of my chains.
Indeed, when he arrived in Rome, he searched diligently until he found me.
May the Lord grant Onesiphorus His mercy on that day. You know very well how much he ministered to me in Ephesus.

anazopyreo.gloss: To rekindle, to fan a fire back into full flame. The picture is of embers that are still alive but need stirring so they blaze again. Paul is not saying Timothy has lost his gift, but that grace-given gifts must be actively stoked, not left to smolder. Faithfulness is not passive; it keeps the coals bright.

deilia.gloss: Timidity, cowardice, a shrinking fearfulness. It is the only place this word appears in the New Testament, and it names the very thing that would keep Timothy quiet and ashamed. Against it Paul sets a threefold gift of the Spirit: power, love, and self-control. The footnote to the verse offers timidity or cowardice as the sense.

epaischynomai.gloss: To be ashamed of, to feel disgrace over. It runs like a thread through the chapter: do not be ashamed of the testimony, I am not ashamed, and Onesiphorus was not ashamed of my chains. In a culture where a chained prisoner was a public embarrassment, Paul calls loyalty to Christ and His servants a matter of holy courage.

hypotyposis.gloss: A pattern, outline, or model to be copied. Timothy is to hold the sound words he heard from Paul as a fixed standard, a form of teaching that shapes everything else. The apostolic gospel is not raw material to be reinvented but a trustworthy template to be preserved and passed on.

paratheke.gloss: A deposit, something valuable placed in another's trust for safekeeping. The same word stands behind what I have entrusted to Him in verse 12 and the treasure entrusted to you in verse 14. God guards what we entrust to Him, and we guard the gospel He entrusts to us; the good deposit is kept safe only with the help of the indwelling Spirit.

basic.context: Paul is in prison in Rome, near the end of his life, writing to Timothy, his younger co-worker whom he loves like a son. Before he gives any hard instructions, he encourages Timothy: he thanks God for the genuine faith Timothy learned from his grandmother and mother, reminds him to stir up God's gift in him, and urges him not to be ashamed of Jesus or of Paul the prisoner, but to be ready to suffer for the good news.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a personal letter, but its call is for every believer: do not be ashamed of Christ, and guard the gospel you have received. Paul intends Timothy to act, to fan the gift into flame and hold the pattern of sound teaching, which shows that faithfulness is something we pursue, leaning on the Holy Spirit rather than on our own nerve.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul tells Timothy to anazopyreo, to fan into flame, the gift of God. He says God did not give a spirit of deilia, timidity or cowardice, but of power, love, and self-control. He is to hold the hypotyposis, the pattern, of sound teaching, and to guard the paratheke, the good deposit, the same word for what Paul has entrusted to God for safekeeping.
basic.typology: Timothy's faith handed down through Lois and Eunice pictures how God preserves His truth across generations. The good deposit guarded by the Spirit looks forward to the whole church keeping the gospel until Christ returns.
basic.christ: Christ Jesus is our Savior who has abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel. This grace was given us in Him before time began and revealed in His appearing. He is the One Paul trusts to guard the deposit safely until that day.
basic.god: God saves and calls us not because of our works but by His own purpose and grace, planned before the ages. He gives His Spirit of power, love, and self-control, and He is able to keep safe everything we entrust to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1 shows Paul unashamed of the gospel because it is God's power for salvation. Ephesians 2 echoes salvation by grace, not works. First Timothy 6 also charges Timothy to guard what has been entrusted to him.
basic.oneStory: God's plan of grace in Christ was set before time began, revealed when Christ appeared, and is now entrusted to His servants to pass on. Timothy stands in a line of faithful people, from Lois and Eunice to Paul, carrying the deposit forward until the day Christ returns.

context.literary: The letter opens with a standard greeting, then a warm thanksgiving that recalls Timothy's godly upbringing. From there Paul builds a case for courage: a reminder about the gift, a statement of the Spirit's character, a summons not to be ashamed, and a grand summary of the gospel of grace in verses 9 and 10. It closes with concrete names, deserters set against the loyal Onesiphorus, giving flesh to the appeal.
context.historical: Paul is a prisoner in Rome under Nero, likely awaiting execution, at a time when association with a condemned Christian carried real danger. Many in the province of Asia have distanced themselves from him. Timothy, younger and by temperament perhaps cautious, ministers in Ephesus amid pressure to soften or hide his allegiance.
context.canonical: As the opening of Paul's final letter, this chapter sets the tone for the Pastoral Epistles' concern to preserve sound doctrine. Verses 9 and 10 stand among the clearest New Testament statements that salvation flows from God's eternal purpose and grace, not human merit.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To strengthen Timothy for faithful, courageous ministry by reminding him of his genuine faith, the Spirit's enabling, the greatness of the gospel of grace, and his duty to guard the apostolic deposit even in the face of suffering and shame.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account of Timothy's family faith and of Onesiphorus is descriptive, held up as example. The commands to fan the gift into flame, not be ashamed, share in suffering, and guard the deposit are prescriptive and abiding for every believer, though shaped to Timothy's particular calling as a minister.

debates.question: What is the gift of God that Timothy must fan into flame?

views.label: A gift for ministry given at his commissioning
views.case: The gift came through the laying on of Paul's hands, pointing to a specific grace for Timothy's pastoral and preaching work, to be stirred up and used.

views.label: The broader empowering of the Spirit for bold service
views.case: Verse 7 immediately names a Spirit of power, love, and self-control, so the gift may be understood as the Spirit's enabling for courageous witness generally.

debates.bearing: Either way the point holds: God-given grace is not to lie dormant; Timothy must actively rekindle and exercise what God has placed in him.

debates.question: In verse 12, what is guarded, and by whom?

views.label: God guards what Timothy and Paul entrust to Him
views.case: The BSB reads that He is able to guard what I have entrusted to Him, so believers commit themselves and their future to God's keeping for that day.

views.label: Paul guards the gospel God entrusted to him
views.case: The footnote allows what He has entrusted to me, making the deposit the gospel itself, which fits the parallel command to guard the deposit in verse 14.

debates.bearing: Both truths are taught in the chapter: God safely keeps those who trust Him, and He calls His servants to guard the gospel entrusted to them by the Spirit's help.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on a cluster of vivid Greek terms. Timothy is to anazopyreo, to fan back into full flame, the gift of God, treating grace like living coals that must be stirred. God has not given a spirit of deilia, timidity or cowardice, the very fear that breeds shame, but power, love, and self-control. Three times the verb epaischynomai, to be ashamed, sounds out: do not be ashamed, I am not ashamed, and Onesiphorus was unashamed of my chains. Timothy must hold the hypotyposis, the fixed pattern, of sound teaching, and guard the paratheke, the good deposit, the same word used for what Paul entrusts to God in verse 12. Trust and stewardship meet in that one word: God keeps what we hand to Him, and we keep what He hands to us.

typology.type: Faith handed down through Lois, Eunice, and Timothy
typology.fulfillment: A picture of God preserving His truth across generations, fulfilled as the church passes the gospel on until Christ returns.

typology.type: The good deposit guarded by the Spirit
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the whole people of God keeping the apostolic gospel intact, a trust kept safe by the indwelling Spirit rather than human strength.

study.christ: The heart of the chapter is Christ Jesus our Savior, who has abolished death and brought life and immortality to light through the gospel. The grace now revealed was given us in Him before time began, then unveiled in His appearing, His first coming to save. Paul suffers unashamed because he knows the One in whom he has believed and is sure Christ is able to guard the deposit for that day, the day of His return. To be ashamed of Christ or of His prisoners is unthinkable when one sees who Christ is and what He has done.
study.god: God is the sovereign author of salvation: He saved us and called us to a holy calling, not because of our works but by His own purpose and grace, granted in Christ before the ages began. He is the God Paul serves with a clear conscience, the giver of a Spirit marked by power, love, and self-control rather than fear. He is faithful to keep what is entrusted to Him and He grants mercy, as Paul prays He will for the household of Onesiphorus on that day.

crossReferences.note: Paul is not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God for salvation, echoing this chapter's call to courage.
Saved by grace through faith, not by works, matching verses 9 and 10.
Guard what has been entrusted to you, the parallel charge to keep the deposit.
He saved us, not by works of righteousness, but according to His mercy, the same gospel of grace.

study.oneStory: Before time began, God purposed to save a people by grace in Christ. That purpose was revealed when the Savior appeared and abolished death, and it is now carried forward as a deposit entrusted to faithful servants. Timothy stands in a living chain, faith passed from Lois and Eunice, gospel passed from Paul, to be guarded and handed on by the Spirit until Christ appears again and grants mercy on that day.

sermon.title: The Good Deposit
sermon.why: Part of Begg's series A Study in 2 Timothy, this message expounds 2 Timothy 1:14-18, the charge to guard the good deposit by the Spirit's help, set against the deserters and the loyal Onesiphorus. A careful, orthodox treatment of the chapter's central theme of faithful stewardship of the gospel.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean when he tells Timothy to fan the gift into flame?

options.text: To actively stir up and use the grace God has given, not let it lie dormant.
options.note: The verb pictures rekindling living coals into full flame; grace must be exercised.

options.text: To wait passively until he feels more confident.
options.note: The image is active stirring, not passive waiting.

options.text: To seek a brand new gift he does not yet have.
options.note: The gift is already in him through the laying on of hands; it needs rekindling, not replacing.

options.text: To keep his gift hidden to avoid trouble.
options.note: The whole point is bold use, the opposite of hiding.

quiz.q: What kind of spirit has God given us, according to verse 7?

options.text: A spirit of timidity that keeps us safe and quiet.
options.note: Paul says God did not give a spirit of fear or cowardice.

options.text: A spirit of power, love, and self-control.
options.note: This is exactly the threefold gift Paul names against timidity.

options.text: A spirit of power only, so we can dominate others.
options.note: Power is named, but alongside love and self-control, not for domination.

options.text: A spirit of love and self-control, but not real power.
options.note: Love and self-control are named, but power is included too.

quiz.q: On what basis does God save and call us, according to verses 9 and 10?

options.text: By His own purpose and grace given in Christ before time began.
options.note: Verse 9 grounds salvation in God's eternal purpose and grace, not our works.

options.text: By our good works and sincere effort.
options.note: Paul explicitly says not because of our works.

options.text: By a mix of grace and our works together.
options.note: Paul rules out works as the basis; it is grace and God's purpose.

options.text: By His grace, revealed through the appearing of Christ our Savior.
options.note: True and central, though the fullest answer names God's eternal purpose behind that grace.

quiz.q: Why is Paul not ashamed even though he suffers in prison?

options.text: Because he knows whom he has believed and trusts Christ to guard the deposit.
options.note: Verse 12 grounds his confidence in the trustworthy Christ he knows.

options.text: Because his imprisonment is only a minor inconvenience.
options.note: Paul takes his suffering seriously; his confidence is in Christ, not in comfort.

options.text: Because he is sure Christ is able to keep what is entrusted for that day.
options.note: True, and part of the same verse, though the anchor is knowing Christ personally.

options.text: Because he expects to be released soon.
options.note: The letter shows Paul expecting death, not release; his hope is Christ.

quiz.q: How does the example of Onesiphorus function in the chapter?

options.text: He models the unashamed loyalty Paul calls for, refreshing Paul and seeking him out in Rome.
options.note: Onesiphorus embodies the courage set against the deserters of Asia.

options.text: He is a warning of how not to treat imprisoned believers.
options.note: He is a positive example, not a warning; the deserters are the warning.

options.text: He shows that some served Paul faithfully while others fell away.
options.note: True, and the contrast is real, though his main role is a model of courage.

options.text: He is praised mainly for his wealth and status.
options.note: He is praised for mercy and refreshing service, not status.

questions: Timothy's faith was nurtured by his grandmother and mother. Who has God used to hand faith down to you, and whom are you helping to pass it on to?
Paul says the gift of God can grow cold if it is not stirred up. Where in your walk with Christ do the coals need fanning back into flame right now?
God gives a Spirit of power, love, and self-control rather than fear. In which situation are you most tempted to shrink back in timidity, and how would those three gifts change your response?
Being ashamed of Christ often shows up in small silences. Where might you be quietly distancing yourself from Jesus or from other believers who are under pressure?
Paul entrusted both his future and the gospel to a God who keeps what is placed in His care. What are you finding hard to entrust to God today, and what would it mean to place it in His keeping?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that the word rendered fear can also be translated timidity or cowardice; it names a shrinking fearfulness, the only occurrence of this Greek term in the New Testament.
The phrase what I have entrusted to Him can also be rendered what He has entrusted to me, as the BSB footnote observes; the two readings highlight God keeping us and us keeping the gospel.
The treasure entrusted translates the good deposit, the same Greek word used in verse 12, linking God's safekeeping of us with our safekeeping of the gospel.
The Province of Asia is literally Asia, a Roman province in what is now western Turkey, as the BSB footnote explains, not the modern continent.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Timothy 1:1-18. Verse 11 note: the poem and line-break formatting in the source has been rendered as clean prose. No em dashes present in this chapter's text.
